Name: jacobian_layer_criticality_routing
Title: Jacobian-Conditioned Layer Criticality: Training-Free Gradient Flow Analysis for Latency-Bounded Inference
Short Hypothesis: Layers with ill-conditioned Jacobian spectral structure (high condition number, rapid singular value decay) contribute minimally to task-specific gradient signal propagation and can be safely skipped at inference time without retraining. By computing layer-wise Jacobian condition numbers via single-pass randomized SVD on a small calibration batch, we can construct a zero-training criticality score that predicts layer redundancy under latency budgets, outperforming activation-geometry-only routing and eliminating the need for learned gating networks.
Related Work: Recent work on dynamic depth (BUDDY, SLEXNet) and training-free routing (SQR, Geometric Signal Separation) focuses on activation covariance geometry or learned decision modules to route computation. However, these approaches decouple gradient flow structure from routing decisions. Stochastic Depth training (Korol et al., 2025) shows that models trained with layer dropout become robust to arbitrary skipping, but does not provide a principled, input-dependent scoring mechanism. Our proposal directly inverts the problem: instead of learning routing gates or using activation statistics, we leverage gradient flow conditioning as a direct, theoretically justified proxy for layer necessity. This bridges PAC-Bayes generalization bounds (which depend on gradient complexity) to system-scale layer selection, filling a gap between mechanistic interpretability and latency optimization.
Abstract: Deep neural network inference remains computationally expensive under strict latency budgets. While recent work has explored activation-geometry-based routing and learned early-exit mechanisms, a fundamental question remains underexplored: which layers are truly critical for task-specific gradient signal propagation? We propose Jacobian-Conditioned Layer Criticality (JCLC), a training-free framework that scores layer necessity by analyzing the spectral structure of layer-wise Jacobians. Our key insight is that layers exhibiting high Jacobian condition numbers and rapid singular value decay contribute minimally to task-relevant gradient flow and can be safely skipped without retraining. We compute criticality scores via single-pass randomized SVD on a small calibration batch (100–500 examples), amortizing cost into a negligible FLOPs budget. These scores feed a deterministic layer-selection policy that enforces strict wall-clock latency constraints. We theoretically ground JCLC in PAC-Bayes bounds, proving that low-rank Jacobian projections preserve generalization guarantees under layer skipping. Empirically, we demonstrate that JCLC achieves superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers compared to activation-only routing (SQR) and learned gating (BUDDY) on vision and language tasks, while maintaining deterministic latency and eliminating per-sample routing overhead. Ablations isolate Jacobian conditioning as the dominant signal, decoupling it from activation statistics. We provide exact hardware telemetry, multi-seed variance quantification, and reproducible artifacts.

Experiments.name: Experiment 1: Jacobian Condition Number as Layer Criticality Proxy
Experiments.description: On CIFAR-10 and ImageNet-100 (ResNet-50, Vision Transformer-B/16), compute layer-wise Jacobian condition numbers κ(J_l) using randomized SVD (rank-50 approximation) on a 256-sample calibration batch. For each layer l, define criticality score as c_l = 1 / (1 + κ(J_l)), mapping high condition numbers to low criticality. Rank layers by criticality and construct layer-skipping configurations (skip bottom 10%, 20%, 30% least critical layers). Compare accuracy-latency trade-offs against: (i) baseline full network, (ii) uniform layer skipping (random), (iii) activation-covariance-based routing (SQR), (iv) learned gating (BUDDY baseline on ResNets). Metric: throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier, wall-clock latency (measured on single GPU with explicit SVD amortization cost), and per-layer gradient signal magnitude (||∂L/∂h_l||_2) to validate that skipped layers have minimal gradient contribution.
Experiments.details: Jacobian computation: For each layer l, sample 10 random vectors v ~ N(0, I) and compute J_l v via backprop, storing as columns of matrix J_l ∈ ℝ^{d_out × 10}. Apply randomized SVD to J_l to extract top-50 singular values σ_1, ..., σ_50. Condition number: κ(J_l) = σ_1 / σ_50. Calibration cost: 256 forward-backward passes + 10 × 256 backprop-vector products, amortized as <2% of inference cost on batch size 32. Latency measurement: wall-clock time on NVIDIA A100 with explicit timing of SVD step, excluding data loading.

Experiments.name: Experiment 2: Stability Under Distribution Shift
Experiments.description: Train criticality scores on clean CIFAR-10 calibration batch. Evaluate layer-skipping policies on: (i) natural distribution shifts (CIFAR-10-C corruption types: blur, noise, brightness), (ii) domain shift (CIFAR-10 → STL-10), (iii) adversarial perturbations (ℓ∞ ε=8/255). For each shift, measure: (a) accuracy retention relative to full network, (b) stability radius of criticality scores (how much Jacobian condition numbers change under perturbation), (c) whether same skipping configuration remains optimal across shifts. Compare against activation-based routing (SQR) which requires recomputing covariance statistics per shift. Metric: accuracy drop, score stability (variance of κ(J_l) across 5 perturbation seeds), and configuration robustness (Kendall τ correlation of layer rankings across clean and shifted data).

Experiments.name: Experiment 3: PAC-Bayes Generalization Bounds Validation
Experiments.description: Derive PAC-Bayes bound linking Jacobian spectral complexity to generalization under layer skipping. Formally: For a layer-skipping policy π that removes layers with κ(J_l) > τ, prove that generalization error is bounded by: ε_gen ≤ ε_train + O(√(∑_l ∈ π κ(J_l) / m)), where m is calibration batch size. Validate bound tightness empirically: measure train-test accuracy gap on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet-100 for varying τ thresholds, plot empirical gap against predicted bound, and measure correlation (R² metric). This validates that condition number is a principled proxy rather than a heuristic.

Experiments.name: Experiment 4: Latency Budget Enforcement
Experiments.description: Implement deterministic layer-selection policy: given target latency budget B (e.g., 50ms), compute expected latency L(π) for each skipping configuration π. Select configuration π* = argmax_π { accuracy(π) : L(π) ≤ B }. Measure: (i) actual wall-clock latency vs. predicted latency (prediction error), (ii) accuracy achieved, (iii) consistency across hardware (NVIDIA A100, RTX 4090, Jetson Orin). Compare against BUDDY (which uses learned budget prediction) and SQR (which does not enforce strict latency). Metric: latency prediction error (absolute and relative), accuracy under budget, and hardware variance (std dev across 3 GPU types).

Experiments.name: Experiment 5: Ablation – Jacobian vs. Activation Geometry
Experiments.description: Isolate Jacobian conditioning as the dominant signal by comparing four routing strategies: (i) Jacobian condition number alone (proposed), (ii) activation covariance condition number (SQR-style), (iii) combined (Jacobian + activation covariance as dual scoring), (iv) random layer ranking (control). For each, construct layer-skipping policies and measure throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier. Use linear regression to quantify contribution of each signal: accuracy = β_0 + β_jac × (Jacobian score) + β_act × (Activation score) + ε. Report standardized coefficients and R² to show Jacobian dominance.

Experiments.name: Experiment 6: Scaling to Large Models (LLMs)
Experiments.description: Apply JCLC to Llama-2-7B and Mistral-7B on language modeling (next-token prediction on WikiText-103 validation). Compute layer-wise Jacobian condition numbers on 256-token calibration batch. Construct layer-skipping policies for inference (skip bottom-k least critical layers). Measure: (i) perplexity under varying k, (ii) latency reduction (tokens/sec), (iii) calibration cost as % of first inference pass. Compare against BUDDY (which supports LLMs). Metric: perplexity-latency Pareto frontier, amortized calibration overhead, and multi-seed variance (5 random calibration batches).

Experiments.name: Experiment 7: Computational Cost Accounting
Experiments.description: Provide exact FLOPs and memory accounting for Jacobian computation. For ResNet-50: (a) forward pass: 4.1G FLOPs, (b) 10 backward-vector products: 41G FLOPs, (c) randomized SVD (rank-50): 0.5G FLOPs. Total: ~45.6G FLOPs amortized over batch size 32 = ~1.4G FLOPs per sample (negligible vs. full inference ~4.1G FLOPs). Memory: store J_l as dense matrix (d_out × 10), peak memory ~100MB for ResNet-50 (well within single GPU). Report wall-clock time on A100: SVD step takes ~50ms, total calibration time ~500ms for 256-sample batch. This validates "negligible overhead" claim with rigorous accounting.

Risk Factors and Limitations: **Jacobian Estimation Variance**: Randomized SVD with rank-50 approximation may underestimate true condition numbers for layers with slow singular value decay. Mitigation: use adaptive rank selection (e.g., rank-k where σ_k / σ_1 > 0.01) and validate against full SVD on small layers.

**Calibration Batch Sensitivity**: Criticality scores computed on a 256-sample batch may not generalize to diverse test distributions. Mitigation: evaluate stability across multiple calibration batches (Experiment 2) and provide sensitivity analysis.

**Limited Applicability to Residual Architectures**: Jacobian condition number may not be predictive in models without strong skip connections (e.g., Transformers with dense attention). Mitigation: include both CNN and Transformer baselines, report per-architecture performance.

**Comparison Fairness**: BUDDY and SQR use trained routing; JCLC is training-free. Direct accuracy comparison may conflate architectural differences. Mitigation: report accuracy-latency Pareto frontiers separately for trained vs. training-free methods, and compare JCLC against training-free baselines (stochastic depth inference, uniform skipping).

**Scalability to Extreme Latency Budgets**: For very tight budgets (e.g., <10ms on edge devices), Jacobian computation overhead (~50ms) becomes prohibitive. Mitigation: amortize over multiple inferences or precompute on development hardware; clearly delineate applicable latency regimes.
**Theoretical Gap**: PAC-Bayes bounds may be loose in practice. Mitigation: empirically validate bound tightness (Experiment 3) and report actual vs. predicted generalization gaps.

**Hardware-Specific Latency Prediction**: Predicted latency depends on hardware characteristics (memory bandwidth, cache hierarchy). Mitigation: measure on multiple GPU types (Experiment 4) and provide hardware-aware latency models.

**Limited to Inference**: Method does not optimize training; only applicable at test time. Mitigation: position as complementary to training-time methods (e.g., distillation, pruning) and clearly scope to inference-only settings.
